subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Bachelor’s Degree from an accredited university
Major field of study was Psychology, with electives in Statistics and Research Methods
Completed capstone project on cognitive processes in social contexts
Attended several short courses on data analysis and qualitative research during undergraduate studies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Social Science Research Assistant
Supports research projects focused on human behavior, cognitive processes, and social interactions
Responsible for data analysis, literature review, participant interaction, writing, and administrative coordination
Collaborates with professionals in neuroscience and clinical psychology

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the academic and research sector
Projects span social science, psychology, and neuroscience disciplines
Organization is research-focused, with occasional collaborations in healthcare and education

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized research institute with approximately 100-200 staff
Work environment is collaborative and interdisciplinary
Frequent interactions across multiple departments and with external academic partners

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Organization is a non-profit research institution affiliated with a university
Focuses on fundamental and applied research in social and cognitive sciences
Secures funding through grants, academic partnerships, and occasional government contracts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has been in the Social Science Research Assistant role for 8 years
Started as a junior assistant and advanced to more complex research responsibilities
Experience spans multiple research projects and grant cycles

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Considered mid-level within the organization's research hierarchy
Acts as a lead assistant on several projects, mentoring newer team members
Has developed specialized expertise in data analysis and research documentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Analyzes quantitative and qualitative data from research studies using statistical software
Conducts literature reviews to inform ongoing and future research projects
Writes and edits research papers, reports, and grant proposals
Interacts with study participants via interviews, surveys, and occasional focus groups
Coordinates meetings with colleagues and external collaborators

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Data analysis: 30% (~10-12 hours per week)
Literature review: 20% (~8-10 hours per week)
Writing/documentation: 20% (~8-10 hours per week)
Meetings/collaboration: 15% (~6-8 hours per week)
Participant interaction: 10% (~4-6 hours per week)
Administrative tasks: 5% (~2 hours per week)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Autonomous in data analysis, literature review, and initial drafting of research documents
Collaborates with principal investigators for research design and major decisions
Empowered to recommend methodological adjustments and propose new research questions
Handles scheduling, small-scale budgeting, and coordination of participant activities independently

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Data cleaning and preparation are routine and efficient due to experience
Literature searches and article organization feel intuitive after years of practice
Drafting research paper outlines comes naturally
Managing reference software and citations is straightforward

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Interpreting ambiguous or borderline statistical results can be complex
Synthesizing large bodies of literature into coherent reviews is demanding
Coordinating cross-departmental research with diverse stakeholders can be challenging
Designing surveys to avoid bias and maximize data quality requires careful work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Bulk data cleaning and formatting in spreadsheets is repetitive
Transcribing interview recordings is time-consuming and suitable for automation
Manually checking citations and building bibliographies is tedious
Distributing and collecting large volumes of survey responses could be automated

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Most enjoys interpreting novel data findings and writing research discussions
Engaged by collaborative meetings that generate new research ideas
Finds repetitive data entry and administrative paperwork tedious
Least enjoys troubleshooting software integration or technical glitches

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry and cleaning are error-prone and time-consuming
Difficulty integrating outputs from different software platforms
Delays in receiving feedback on draft research papers from colleagues
Limited automation for qualitative data analysis creates workflow bottlenecks

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: High skill and enjoyment in data analysis leads to greater productivity and faster insights
Feels more productive when tasks are intellectually stimulating and collaborative
Tedious, low-skill tasks tend to result in lower engagement and slower completion
Enjoyment increases when using tools that reduce manual workload (e.g., reference managers)

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Has a proactive attitude toward adopting new research technologies
Views technological change as an opportunity to increase efficiency
Open to experimenting with new tools if they show clear benefits

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to software updates and new platforms
Comfortable self-training on new statistical and reference management tools
Able to transition workflow when organizational tools change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours per month to learning new research technologies
Attends optional workshops or webinars if relevant to current projects
Prefers hands-on, project-based learning over theoretical courses

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the prospect of reducing repetitive workload
More likely to adopt tools that directly improve data analysis or writing
High current workload can be a barrier to exploring less immediately relevant technologies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers’ positive experiences with tools often influence adoption decisions
Management encourages experimentation with productivity-enhancing software
Team success stories accelerate willingness to try new platforms

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses SPSS and R for statistical analysis on a near-daily basis
Manages references with Zotero and Mendeley
Administers surveys with SurveyMonkey and Google Forms
Relies on Microsoft Excel for data cleaning and visualization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Microsoft Word and Google Docs for drafting research documents
Slack and Zoom for team communication and virtual meetings
Trello for basic project management and tracking research tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient with Qualtrics and REDCap for advanced survey administration
Experienced with NVivo for qualitative data analysis (used occasionally)
Familiar with EndNote for publication-level citation management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally faces issues transferring data between SPSS and Excel
Integrating citation output from Zotero into non-standard journal templates can be problematic
Survey data exported from Google Forms sometimes requires manual reformatting for analysis
Collaboration tools may not always sync well with institutional email systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Comfortable with basic concepts like machine learning, natural language processing, and predictive analytics
Understands the distinction between supervised and unsupervised learning
Familiar with AI terminology relevant to qualitative and quantitative research

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT and similar LLMs for literature summarization and drafting
Employs automated transcription tools like Otter.ai for interview recordings
Occasionally leverages NVivo’s AI-assisted coding for qualitative data
Utilizes Grammarly’s AI features for writing refinement

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses LLMs daily for summarizing research articles and generating first drafts
Employs AI transcription tools several times a week to convert audio interviews to text
Applies AI-powered search assistants for literature discovery at the start of new projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: AI-generated literature summaries have accelerated review processes, saving several hours per week
Automated transcriptions occasionally miss domain-specific terminology, requiring manual correction
LLMs sometimes generate plausible but inaccurate citations, requiring verification
AI-based qualitative coding can misinterpret context without careful supervision

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Organization offers optional lunchtime seminars on AI in research
Peers often share tips and best practices for maximizing LLM output quality
Written guides for integrating AI into research workflows are available on the internal knowledge base

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools for complex writing or analysis tasks
Uses LLMs independently for quick summaries or brainstorming
Appreciates the ability to iterate interactively with AI to refine outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI primarily as an assistant for drafting and summarization tasks
Occasionally treats AI as an advisor when exploring unfamiliar research topics
Relies on AI as a co-worker in repetitive or large-scale data cleaning processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to adopting AI workflows that reduce manual workload
Quick to test and integrate new AI features into survey design and analysis
Regularly experiments with new AI tools to assess fit with current tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming and literature review
Uses command-based AI features for specific, repeatable tasks like data formatting
Values flexibility to switch between interface styles depending on task complexity

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Interpreting research findings and drawing conclusions requires deep subject knowledge
Survey design and qualitative coding depend on nuanced understanding of human behavior
Manual oversight is essential for tasks involving participant welfare or ethics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI for routine tasks like summarization and transcription, but always verifies outputs
Relies on human review for critical analyses and final research conclusions
Skeptical of AI-generated citations or literature reviews without manual validation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI to handle repetitive, low-value components, preserving human focus for interpretation and synthesis
Sees AI as augmenting, not replacing, expert judgment in research design and analysis
Advocates for shared oversight on tasks involving sensitive or novel data

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation for data cleaning, transcription, and citation management
Finds automation threatening when it could obscure methodological transparency or introduce unchecked bias
Reserves manual control for tasks with ethical or participant-impact implications

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances the value of analytical and interpretive skills by automating low-level work
Routine writing and data preparation skills are less critical due to automation
Critical thinking and methodological rigor have become more important in the AI era

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing prompt engineering and effective AI query skills
Monitoring and evaluating AI output accuracy is a new responsibility
Adapting workflows to leverage new AI features is now part of the role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Minimal concern about AI replacing core analytical or interpretive tasks
Recognizes some administrative and data processing tasks may be fully automated
Views AI as a tool to increase research capacity rather than as a threat to job security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI outputs are subject to manual review before inclusion in research products
Can edit and override AI-generated text, summaries, and citations at any time
Institutional policies require human sign-off on critical research deliverables

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team has adopted guidelines for responsible and transparent AI use
Recent updates to data privacy policies explicitly address the use of AI tools
Management encourages ongoing evaluation of AI’s impact on workflow and quality

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a positive force for accelerating scientific discovery and knowledge dissemination
Cautious about potential risks related to bias and misinformation
Believes AI will increasingly transform research workflows and data analysis

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Advocates for transparency in how AI tools are used in research
Concerned about data privacy and the ethical use of participant information
Believes human oversight is essential to prevent unintended consequences

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from automated data cleaning tools to handle large, messy datasets
Desires predictive analytics tailored to complex social science data
Sees a need for intelligent literature search assistants that understand nuanced research questions
Wants seamless AI-assisted citation management integrated with writing platforms

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates a shift towards more interpretive and supervisory research roles
Expects routine data processing and reporting to become largely automated
Predicts increased collaboration with AI systems for designing and analyzing studies
Foresees new roles in managing and validating AI-driven research outputs

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Develop clear protocols for responsible AI use in research projects
Invest in ongoing staff training to keep pace with evolving AI tools
Encourage pilot projects to evaluate new AI applications before full adoption
Regularly review ethical implications and update policies as AI capabilities advance
